stem: Consider the linear equation \( x\,y' + [[a]]y = g(x) \) on \( x > 0 \). Written in standard form \( y' + P(x)y = Q(x) \), its integrating factor is \( \mu(x) = e^{\int P(x)\,dx} \). What is \( \mu([[t0]]) \)?
solution: In standard form \( P(x) = \frac{[[a]]}{x} \), so \( \int P\,dx = [[a]]\ln x \) and \( \mu(x) = e^{[[a]]\ln x} = x^{[[a]]} \). Therefore \( \mu([[t0]]) = [[t0]]^{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \mu = e^{[[a]]x} \), forgetting that \( P(x) = [[a]]/x \), not \( [[a]] \).
This uses the wrong sign, giving \( \mu = x^{-[[a]]} \).
This exponentiates the wrong base, computing \( [[a]]^{[[t0]]} \).
This multiplies instead of exponentiating.
This uses an exponent that is one too large.
This uses an exponent that is one too small.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Consider the constant-coefficient linear equation \( y' + [[a]]y = g(x) \), already in standard form \( y' + P(x)y = Q(x) \). Its integrating factor is \( \mu(x) = e^{\int P(x)\,dx} \). What is \( \mu([[t0]]) \)?
solution: Here \( P(x) = [[a]] \) is constant, so \( \int P\,dx = [[a]]x \) and \( \mu(x) = e^{[[a]]x} \). Therefore \( \mu([[t0]]) = e^{[[a]]\cdot[[t0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the exponent.
This confuses the constant-coefficient case with the power-law case \( \mu = x^{a} \).
This multiplies by \( t_0 \) after exponentiating instead of putting it in the exponent.
This forgets to exponentiate entirely.
This uses one fewer unit in the exponent.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is twice the correct value.

stem: Consider the linear equation \( y' + [[a]]x\,y = g(x) \), already in standard form \( y' + P(x)y = Q(x) \) with \( P(x) = [[a]]x \). Its integrating factor is \( \mu(x) = e^{\int P(x)\,dx} \). What is \( \mu([[t0]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2 when integrating \( [[a]]x \).
This treats \( P(x) \) as constant, dropping the factor of \( x \) from the integration.
This flips the sign of the exponent.
This confuses this case with the power-law integrating factor \( x^{a}\).
This forgets to square \( t_0 \) in the exponent.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is twice the correct value.

stem: For a first-order linear equation \( y' + P(x)y = Q(x) \), the integrating factor is found to be \( \mu(x) = x^{[[n]]} \) (for \( x > 0 \)). What is \( P([[t0]]) \)?
solution: Since \( \mu(x) = e^{\int P\,dx} = x^{[[n]]} = e^{[[n]]\ln x} \), we have \( \int P\,dx = [[n]]\ln x \), so \( P(x) = \dfrac{[[n]]}{x} \). Therefore \( P([[t0]]) = \dfrac{[[n]]}{[[t0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, dividing \( t_0 \) by \( n \) instead of \( n \) by \( t_0 \).
This has the wrong sign on \( P(x) \).
This multiplies instead of dividing.
This uses one more than the correct exponent.
This uses one more than the correct evaluation point in the denominator.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
